// SLIDE 01 — HOOK

CHAPTER 6 — EXTRACTING DATA FROM DOCUMENTS

Extraction turns documents into structured data; the structure can make errors look official. Define your schema before

NARRATION

Extraction turns documents into structured data; the structure can make errors look official. Define your schema before you ask Cowork to extract anything. Source traceability , every row tied to its source file , is not optional. Verify row counts, spot-check numeric fields, and log ambiguous cases rather than guessing. Clean-looking output is not the same as correct output.

// SLIDE 02 — THE SCENE

THE SCENE

You have forty-seven receipts from the department's summer field work. Some are PDFs from vendors with clean digital lay

The Scene — You need a spreadsheet: vendor, date, total, tax, category, and source file for each one.
NARRATION

You have forty-seven receipts from the department's summer field work. Some are PDFs from vendors with clean digital layouts. Several are photos taken on a phone in bad light. Two have handwritten notes scrawled over the total line. One is a crumpled scan that came through the mail. You need a spreadsheet: vendor, date, total, tax, category, and source file for each one.

// SLIDE 03 — EXTRACTION VS. SUMMARIZATION

EXTRACTION VS. SUMMARIZATION

Summarization→Extraction→structure confers credibility
NARRATION

These two operations look similar but create different risks. Summarization produces a condensed account of what a document says. The reader understands they are getting an interpretation, not a transcription. Some detail is expected to disappear.

// SLIDE 04 — THE SCHEMA COMES FIRST

THE SCHEMA COMES FIRST.

Fieldswhat columns exist, by name
Typestext, date, currency, number, category
Source locationwhere in the document this field typically lives
Validation ruleswhat a valid value looks like
Exception ruleswhat to do when the field is missing, ambiguous, unreadable, or uncertain
NARRATION

The most common extraction mistake is asking Cowork to extract data before deciding what the data is. Fields: what columns exist, by name Types: text, date, currency, number, category Source location: where in the document this field typically lives Validation rules: what a valid value looks like Exception rules: what to do when the field is missing, ambiguous, unreadable, or uncertain

// SLIDE 05 — A WORKED WALKTHROUGH

A WORKED WALKTHROUGH.

Vendortext from receipt header; mark "unclear" if unreadable
Datedate of purchase from receipt; do not use file metadata as substitute; mark "ambiguous" if uncertain
Totaltotal amount from printed total line; currency in USD; do not infer from line items; mark "uncertain" if unreadable
Taxtax amount if shown; leave blank if absent; do not substitute zero
Categoryone of ; mark "uncategorized" if unknown
NARRATION

The task: Extract expense data from forty-seven receipt files in a folder called field-receipts-summer-2026/. Output a spreadsheet with one row per receipt. Copy the receipt folder into your Cowork working directory. Do not work on originals. Restrict Cowork's access to the working copy only (Anthropic, "Use Claude Cowork safely" ).

// SLIDE 06 — THE VERIFICATION CHECKLIST

THE VERIFICATION CHECKLIST.

Row count first.→Source file column.→Exception log review.→Numeric spot-check.→Totals reconciliation.
NARRATION

Row count first. If you had forty-seven receipts, you should have forty-seven rows , or fewer rows plus exception entries that explain the difference. A row count mismatch means a receipt was silently dropped. Source file column. Every row must have a source file name. Any row missing this field means you cannot go back to the original. That row is unverifiable and should be flagged before the data is used.

// SLIDE 07 — HOW MUCH TO VERIFY

HOW MUCH TO VERIFY.

Full row-by-row verification→Risk-tiered sampling→Never skip verification entirely.
NARRATION

The verification question is not "do I check or not." It is "how much checking fits the stakes." Full row-by-row verification is appropriate when: the data feeds financial records, the source documents are high-risk (invoices, legal documents, regulated forms), the extraction involved many image files, or any verification sample reveals errors.

// SLIDE 08 — THE HUMAN GATE

THE HUMAN GATE.

·Row count matches source count (or exceptions explain the difference).
·Every row has a source file reference.
·You have opened and verified a meaningful sample against originals.
·All uncertain fields are either resolved or clearly marked.
·Numeric totals are consistent with any independent cross-check.
NARRATION

Before you use the extracted data for anything , sending it to accounting, loading it into a report, sharing it with a supervisor , confirm: Row count matches source count (or exceptions explain the difference). Every row has a source file reference. You have opened and verified a meaningful sample against originals. All uncertain fields are either resolved or clearly marked. Numeric totals are consistent with any independent cross-check. The output is a new file; the source documents are untouched.

// SLIDE 09 — COMMON MISTAKES

COMMON MISTAKES

Skipping the schema.→Treating clean formatting as accuracy.→Using file metadata as a date.→Not requiring an exception log.→Spot-checking only easy rows.
NARRATION

Skipping the schema. Without defined fields and exception rules, Cowork invents them. The resulting table may look consistent while hiding significant variation in how fields were interpreted. Treating clean formatting as accuracy. A professionally formatted spreadsheet with column headers is not evidence of correct extraction. It is evidence that Cowork could produce a table. The values may still be wrong.

// SLIDE 10 — BRIDGE TO CHAPTER 7

BRIDGE TO CHAPTER 7.

You have extracted the data. Now someone needs to read it.

Bridge to Chapter 7 — The next chapter covers the other direction of Cowork document work: not taking structured data out of documents, but as
NARRATION

You have extracted the data. Now someone needs to read it. The next chapter covers the other direction of Cowork document work: not taking structured data out of documents, but assembling source materials into reports and presentations. The risks shift. You will not be checking decimal points. You will be checking whether polished prose accurately represents evidence , and whether a clean-looking executive summary is saying something true.

// SLIDE 11 — AI WAYBACK MACHINE

AI WAYBACK MACHINE.

Katherine Johnson→Run this:→"Katherine Johnson mathematician"→Now make the prompt better.
NARRATION

Katherine Johnson spent years at NASA's Langley Research Center doing what the agency called "computing" , checking and independently verifying the orbital calculations that engineers and early computers produced. Her verification was not redundant; it was the mechanism by which the calculations became trustworthy enough to stake lives on. She did not distrust the systems she checked. She understood that trust is built through checking, not assumed from clean output.

// SLIDE 12 — THESIS

THE CORE CLAIM.

Katherine Johnson spent years at NASA's Langley Research Center doing what the agency called "computing" — checking and

NARRATION

Katherine Johnson spent years at NASA's Langley Research Center doing what the agency called "computing" , checking and independently verifying the orbital calculations that engineers and early computers produced. Her verification was not redundant; it was the mechanism by which the calculations became trustworthy enough to stake lives on. She did not distrust the systems she checked. She understood that trust is built through checking, not assumed from clean output.

// SLIDE 13 — CLOSE
